title: Archangel Michael (Monthly) — St. Epiphanius of Cyprus & St. Germanus of Constantinople
narrative:
The Holy Archangel Michael the Commander of the Heavenly Hosts, St. Epiphanius of Cyprus the Bishop and Theologian, and St. Germanus of Constantinople the Patriarch and Confessor

On the twelfth of Ginbot, the holy Church celebrates the monthly commemoration of the holy Archangel Michael, the Commander of the Heavenly Hosts, the Prince of the Presence, the one whose name is a declaration of faith — Mi-ka-el, Who is like God? — and whose ministry in the Scriptures spans from the guarding of Eden to the defence of Israel to the struggle with the devil over the body of Moses to the appearance in Daniel to the war in heaven of the Apocalypse. Michael is the archangel of divine justice, the one who executes the judgments that mercy requires, the one who stands at the threshold between the created and uncreated worlds and maintains the order that makes human salvation possible. In Ethiopia, Michael holds a place of honour second in the angelic hierarchy only to God himself: hundreds of churches bear his name, the twelfth of every month is his feast, and the tradition understands his intercession as the most direct angelic mediation available to the faithful.

The theology of Michael in the Ethiopian tradition is centred on his role as the guardian of souls at the moment of death. The tradition preserves a rich iconographic and liturgical vision in which Michael accompanies each departing soul, defends it against the accusations of the adversary, and presents it before the divine throne — a role that connects him intimately with the Paschal mystery, since it is the resurrection of Christ that gives Michael the authority to make this presentation, the empty tomb being the basis on which every subsequent soul can be offered to God with confidence. To celebrate Michael's monthly feast in Ginbot is to celebrate this advocacy in the fullness of the Paschal season, to declare that the victory of the Resurrection extends through Michael's ministry to each individual Christian soul in the moment of its greatest vulnerability.

Saint Epiphanius of Cyprus is one of the great and complex figures of the fourth century: a bishop of Salamis who spent fifty years combating every heresy he could identify, whose encyclopaedic work the Panarion — the Medicine Chest — catalogued and refuted more than eighty heresies, and whose zeal for doctrinal purity led him into controversies that his admirers have sometimes had difficulty defending, including a late-life conflict with John Chrysostom. Yet the tradition retains him as a saint not despite his excesses but because of the fundamental orientation of his life: a man who had memorised the entire Scripture by the time he was a young monk, who carried that memorisation into every controversy, and who understood the defence of doctrinal boundaries as the same act of love that Paphnutius had performed at Nicaea — the act of preserving the space within which genuine encounter with the living God remains possible. Epiphanius erred in detail but not in direction, and the Church commemorates his direction.

Saint Germanus of Constantinople is the great discovery of this day: one of the most important figures in the history of Byzantine liturgical theology, whose commentary on the Divine Liturgy — the Historia Ecclesiastica — is the foundational document of the allegorical interpretation of the liturgical actions that has shaped Orthodox liturgical theology from the eighth century to the present day. Germanus served as Patriarch of Constantinople in the early eighth century during the first outbreak of Byzantine iconoclasm, and when the Emperor Leo III ordered the removal of icons from churches, Germanus refused, was deposed, and spent the remainder of his life in exile, writing in defence of the veneration of icons in terms that anticipate the later and more systematic defence of Theodore the Studite and John of Damascus. His life alongside Michael the archangel-defender and Epiphanius the heresy-cataloguer is the tradition's meditation on three forms of the same fidelity: the angelic defence of divine order, the theological defence of doctrinal boundaries, and the episcopal defence of liturgical beauty — all three expressions of the same love for the God who cannot be confined to categories but who wills to be known through the forms he has given his Church. Amen.
